section.title: The Bread of Life
section.sectionRationale: The fourth and fifth signs, the feeding of the five thousand and the walking on water, set up the Gospel's first great I am discourse. Jesus refuses the crowd's bread-king enthusiasm and points them past perishing food to Himself, the true bread from heaven. The teaching that His flesh, given for the life of the world, must be eaten by faith proves so hard that many disciples turn back, leaving the Twelve, through Peter, to confess that He alone has the words of eternal life.
section.thesis: Jesus is the true bread from heaven, greater than the manna, who gives His flesh for the life of the world, so that everyone the Father gives Him and who comes and feeds on Him by faith has eternal life and will be raised at the last day, a teaching that sifts the crowd and reveals who truly believes.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the feeding of the five thousand; the walking on the water and the words It is I; the discourse on the true bread from heaven and I am the bread of life; the hard saying about eating His flesh and drinking His blood; and the sifting, as many turn back and Peter confesses the words of eternal life.

units.label: The feeding of the five thousand
units.note: the fourth sign, bread in the wilderness

verses.text: After this, Jesus crossed to the other side of the Sea of Galilee (that is, the Sea of Tiberias).
A large crowd followed Him because they saw the signs He was performing on the sick.
Then Jesus went up on the mountain and sat down with His disciples.
Now the Jewish Feast of the Passover was near.
When Jesus looked up and saw a large crowd coming toward Him, He said to Philip, “Where can we buy bread for these people to eat?”
But He was asking this to test him, for He knew what He was about to do.
Philip answered, “Two hundred denarii would not buy enough bread for each of them to have a small piece.”
One of His disciples, Andrew, Simon Peter’s brother, said to Him,
“Here is a boy with five barley loaves and two small fish. But what difference will these make among so many?”
“Have the people sit down,” Jesus said. Now there was plenty of grass in that place, so the men sat down, about five thousand of them.
Then Jesus took the loaves and the fish, gave thanks, and distributed to those who were seated as much as they wanted.
And when everyone was full, He said to His disciples, “Gather the pieces that are left over, so that nothing will be wasted.”
So they collected them and filled twelve baskets with the pieces of the five barley loaves left over by those who had eaten.
When the people saw the sign that Jesus had performed, they began to say, “Truly this is the Prophet who is to come into the world.”
Then Jesus, realizing that they were about to come and make Him king by force, withdrew again to a mountain by Himself.

units.label: Jesus walks on water
units.note: It is I; do not be afraid

verses.text: When evening came, His disciples went down to the sea,
got into a boat, and started across the sea to Capernaum. It was already dark, and Jesus had not yet gone out to them.
A strong wind was blowing, and the sea grew agitated.
When they had rowed about three or four miles, they saw Jesus approaching the boat, walking on the sea, and they were terrified.
But Jesus spoke up: “It is I; do not be afraid.”
Then they were willing to take Him into the boat, and at once the boat reached the shore where they were heading.

units.label: The true bread from heaven
units.note: I am the bread of life

verses.text: The next day, the crowd that had remained on the other side of the sea realized that only one boat had been there, and that Jesus had not boarded it with His disciples, but they had gone away alone.
However, some boats from Tiberias landed near the place where the people had eaten the bread after the Lord had given thanks.
So when the crowd saw that neither Jesus nor His disciples were there, they got into the boats and went to Capernaum to look for Him.
When they found Him on the other side of the sea, they asked Him, “Rabbi, when did You get here?”
Jesus replied, “Truly, truly, I tell you, it is not because you saw these signs that you are looking for Me, but because you ate the loaves and had your fill.
Do not work for food that perishes, but for food that endures to {{zoe|eternal life}}, which the Son of Man will give you. For on Him God the Father has placed His seal of approval.”
Then they inquired, “What must we do to perform the works of God?”
Jesus replied, “The work of God is this: to believe in the One He has sent.”
So they asked Him, “What sign then will You perform, so that we may see it and believe You? What will You do?
Our fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, as it is written: ‘He gave them bread from heaven to eat.’”
Jesus said to them, “Truly, truly, I tell you, it was not Moses who gave you the bread from heaven, but it is My Father who gives you the true bread from heaven.
For the bread of God is He who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world.”
“Sir,” they said, “give us this bread at all times.”
Jesus answered, “{{egoeimi|I am}} the {{artos|bread of life}}. Whoever comes to Me will never hunger, and whoever believes in Me will never thirst.
But as I stated, you have seen Me and still you do not believe.
Everyone the Father gives Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me I will never drive away.
For I have come down from heaven, not to do My own will, but to do the will of Him who sent Me.
And this is the will of Him who sent Me, that I shall lose none of those He has given Me, but raise them up at the last day.
For it is My Father’s will that everyone who looks to the Son and believes in Him shall have eternal life, and I will raise him up at the last day.”

units.label: The bread is My flesh
units.note: given for the life of the world

verses.text: At this, the Jews began to grumble about Jesus because He had said, “I am the bread that came down from heaven.”
They were asking, “Is this not Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? How then can He say, ‘I have come down from heaven?’”
“Stop grumbling among yourselves,” Jesus replied.
“No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him, and I will raise him up at the last day.
It is written in the Prophets: ‘And they will all be taught by God.’ Everyone who has heard the Father and learned from Him comes to Me,
not that anyone has seen the Father except the One who is from God; only He has seen the Father.
Truly, truly, I tell you, he who believes has eternal life.
I am the bread of life.
Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, yet they died.
This is the bread that comes down from heaven, so that anyone may eat of it and not die.
I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever. And this bread, which I will give for the life of the world, is My {{sarx|flesh}}.”
At this, the Jews began to argue among themselves, “How can this man give us His flesh to eat?”
So Jesus said to them, “Truly, truly, I tell you, unless you eat the flesh and drink the blood of the Son of Man, you have no life in you.
Whoever eats My flesh and drinks My blood has eternal life, and I will raise him up at the last day.
For My flesh is real food, and My blood is real drink.
Whoever eats My flesh and drinks My blood {{meno|remains}} in Me, and I in him.
Just as the living Father sent Me and I live because of the Father, so also the one who feeds on Me will live because of Me.
This is the bread that came down from heaven. Unlike your fathers, who ate the manna and died, the one who eats this bread will live forever.”

units.label: Many turn back; Peter confesses
units.note: Lord, to whom would we go?

verses.text: Jesus said this while teaching in the synagogue in Capernaum.
On hearing it, many of His disciples said, “This is a difficult teaching. Who can accept it?”
Aware that His disciples were grumbling about this teaching, Jesus asked them, “Does this offend you?
Then what will happen if you see the Son of Man ascend to where He was before?
The Spirit gives life; the flesh profits nothing. The words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life.
However, there are some of you who do not believe.” (For Jesus had known from the beginning which of them did not believe and who would betray Him.)
Then Jesus said, “This is why I told you that no one can come to Me unless the Father has granted it to him.”
From that time on many of His disciples turned back and no longer walked with Him.
So Jesus asked the Twelve, “Do you want to leave too?”
Simon Peter replied, “Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life.
We believe and know that You are the Holy One of God.”
Jesus answered them, “Have I not chosen you, the Twelve? Yet one of you is a devil!”
He was speaking about Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot. For although Judas was one of the Twelve, he was later to betray Jesus.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. Here begins the great series of I am sayings in John, in which Jesus completes the phrase with an image, I am the bread of life. The words echo the divine name, I AM, revealed to Moses; on the sea Jesus uses the phrase absolutely, It is I, literally I am, do not be afraid. Each saying reveals who Jesus is and what He gives.

artos.gloss: Bread, food, the basic staple of life. Jesus takes the miracle of loaves and the memory of manna and declares Himself the true bread from heaven, the bread of God who gives life to the world. Physical bread sustains for a day and cannot conquer death; this bread, eaten by faith, gives life forever.

zoe.gloss: Life, especially eternal life, the life of God and of the age to come. In this chapter it is contrasted with food that perishes; the true bread endures to eternal life. To have this life is to be raised up at the last day, so that eternal life embraces both a present possession by faith and a future resurrection.

sarx.gloss: Flesh. The bread Jesus gives for the life of the world is His flesh, a plain pointer to His death on the cross, His body given up. To eat His flesh and drink His blood is to receive by faith the crucified Christ and all the benefits of His death. Jesus adds that the Spirit gives life and the flesh, understood in a merely physical way, profits nothing.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, stay. Whoever feeds on Christ by faith remains in Him, and He in them, a mutual indwelling that will be unfolded fully in the vine and the branches. To abide is not a fleeting contact but a settled, living union with Christ from which life continually flows.

basic.context: Near Passover, Jesus feeds a crowd of five thousand from a boy's five loaves and two fish, with twelve baskets left over. The crowd wants to make Him king by force, so He withdraws, and that night He comes to the disciples walking on the sea, saying, It is I, do not be afraid. The next day the crowd finds Him, still hungry for more bread, and Jesus turns them from perishing food to Himself, the true bread from heaven. When He says the bread is His flesh, given for the world, and that they must eat it, many are offended and leave. Peter, for the Twelve, says, Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life.
basic.hermeneutics: Jesus keeps moving people from the physical to the spiritual: from loaves to the bread of life, from manna to Himself. The key question is what it means to eat this bread, and Jesus answers plainly, whoever comes to Me and believes. Eating His flesh and drinking His blood is vivid language for receiving the crucified Christ by faith. This is a hard teaching that sifts the crowd; the chapter shows that true faith is a gift the Father gives, and that it clings to Christ even when the teaching is difficult.
basic.originalLanguages: The discourse turns on a few words. Jesus says ego eimi, I am, the bread of life, the first named I am saying. He is the true artos, the bread from heaven, greater than the manna. He gives His sarx, His flesh, for the life of the world, pointing to the cross. To feed on Him is to have zoe, eternal life, and to meno, to remain, in Him and He in us.
basic.typology: The manna in the wilderness, bread from heaven that could not conquer death, foreshadows the true bread, Christ, who gives life forever. The Passover setting points to the Lamb whose flesh is given for the world. The feeding on a mountain recalls God spreading a table for His people.
basic.christ: Christ is the true bread from heaven who comes down to give life to the world. He walks on the sea and speaks the divine It is I. He gives His flesh in death for the life of the world, and He keeps every one the Father gives Him, raising them at the last day. He alone has the words of eternal life.
basic.god: The Father gives the true bread, draws people to the Son, and grants them to come. His will is that the Son lose none but raise them at the last day. Salvation is His gracious initiative from beginning to end, even as it calls for real faith.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 16 and Psalm 78 recount the manna. Isaiah 54 promises that all will be taught by God. Deuteronomy 8 says man does not live by bread alone. The Lord's Supper, in the other Gospels, echoes eating Christ's body and blood.
basic.oneStory: The God who fed Israel with manna now gives the true bread from heaven, His own Son, whose flesh is given for the life of the world. The Passover points to the Lamb, and the hard call to feed on Christ divides the crowd, gathering a people who, drawn by the Father, cling to the one who has the words of eternal life.

context.literary: Two signs, the feeding and the sea-crossing, are followed by the bread of life discourse, which interprets them, the classic sign-and-discourse pattern. The discourse escalates from bread to the true bread to Jesus' flesh, provoking successive waves of grumbling, argument, and finally desertion. The chapter is framed by Passover at the start and the naming of the betrayer at the end, and it climaxes in the first of the I am sayings.
context.historical: The feeding near Passover, with Jesus on a mountain and talk of manna, evokes Moses and the exodus, feeding the crowd's hope for a prophet-king like Moses who would give bread. Manna was the defining wilderness provision, remembered every Passover season. Walking on the sea recalls God's mastery over the waters in the Old Testament. Synagogue teaching in Capernaum is the setting for the discourse's later, harder stages.
context.canonical: The bread of life discourse stands with the exodus manna tradition, Exodus 16 and Psalm 78:24, which it fulfills. Its language of eating Christ's flesh and drinking His blood has long been read alongside the Lord's Supper, though its primary sense is receiving the crucified Christ by faith. The chapter's teaching on the Father's drawing and giving, and the Son losing none, feeds the New Testament's doctrine of grace, election, and the perseverance of believers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus as the true bread from heaven who gives His flesh for the life of the world, to call for the response of coming to Him and believing, and to show how His hard teaching sifts genuine faith from mere enthusiasm, so that true disciples cling to the one with the words of eternal life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes particular signs and a particular sifting. Its abiding teaching is prescriptive: Jesus is the only bread that gives eternal life, and He must be received by faith; the promise that the Son loses none of those the Father gives is a permanent comfort to believers.

debates.question: What does eating Jesus' flesh and drinking His blood mean?

views.label: Receiving the crucified Christ by faith
views.case: The discourse equates eating and drinking with coming to Jesus and believing, verses 35 and 47, and Jesus says the Spirit gives life and the flesh profits nothing, verse 63; so the eating is a vivid metaphor for faith that receives Christ and His death.

views.label: A pointer that includes the Lord's Supper
views.case: The strongly physical language of flesh and blood, real food and real drink, has led many to hear an anticipation of the Lord's Supper, in which believers feed on Christ; the sign and the reality belong together, though faith remains the means.

debates.bearing: Both agree that the life is received only by faith in the crucified Christ; whether or not the Supper is in view, no ritual saves apart from the believing reception of Jesus Himself.

debates.question: How do the Father's drawing and the human response fit together?

views.label: The Father's sovereign grace is decisive
views.case: No one can come unless the Father draws and grants it, verses 44 and 65, and everyone the Father gives will come, verse 37; coming to Christ rests on the Father's gracious initiative.

views.label: Genuine human coming and believing are real
views.case: Jesus still calls all to come and believe and holds unbelief culpable; the drawing does not cancel responsibility but enables the real response of faith, verse 40.

debates.bearing: John holds both without embarrassment: the Father graciously draws and gives a people to the Son, and those so drawn truly come and believe and are kept to the last day.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter unfolds through key terms. On the water Jesus says ego eimi, literally I am, do not be afraid, and in the synagogue He completes the phrase, I am the bread of life, the first named I am saying. He is the true artos, bread from heaven, surpassing the manna that could not defeat death. The bread He gives is His sarx, His flesh, for the life of the world, a plain pointer to the cross; to feed on Him by faith is to have zoe, eternal life, and to be raised at the last day. And such a one comes to meno, to remain, in Christ, and Christ in him, a living union that is the source of all life.

typology.type: The manna, bread from heaven, Exodus 16 and Psalm 78:24
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true bread from heaven that gives eternal life, unlike the manna whose eaters died, John 6:32-33 and 49-51.
Christ, whose flesh is given for the life of the world, feeding His people by His sacrificial death near the Passover, John 6:4 and 51.
Jesus, greater than Moses, who gives not merely bread in the wilderness but Himself as the bread of life, Deuteronomy 18:15 and John 6:14.

study.christ: Christ reveals Himself as the true bread from heaven, the one who comes down to give life to the world. He masters the sea and speaks the divine It is I, do not be afraid. He is the bread of life, so that whoever comes to Him will never hunger and whoever believes will never thirst. He gives His very flesh in death for the life of the world, and He pledges to lose none of those the Father gives Him but to raise them at the last day. When the crowds fall away, He remains the only one who has the words of eternal life, the Holy One of God confessed by Peter.
study.god: The Father is the giver of the true bread and the one who seals the Son with His approval. He draws people to the Son, grants them to come, and gives a people to Christ, whose settled will is that the Son lose none of them but raise them up. Salvation is thus the Father's gracious work from first to last, a drawing that produces real faith. The Father is also the living Father, the source of the Son's life, so that those who feed on the Son live because of Him.

crossReferences.note: The LORD rained down bread from heaven, the manna that the true bread from heaven fulfills and surpasses.
He gave them grain from heaven to eat, the text the crowd quotes about the manna.
All your children will be taught by the LORD, which Jesus applies to those the Father draws to Him.
Man does not live by bread alone, pointing beyond physical food to the God who is the true sustenance.

study.oneStory: The God who fed Israel with manna in the wilderness now gives the true bread from heaven, His own Son, whose flesh is given for the life of the world. Set at Passover, the discourse points to the Lamb whose death feeds a starving world. The hard call to feed on Christ sifts the crowd, exposing shallow enthusiasm and gathering a people whom the Father draws, who come and believe and are kept to the last day. When many turn back, the true disciples voice the heart of faith: Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life.

quiz.q: What does Jesus want the crowd to seek after the feeding?

options.text: Not perishing food, but the food that endures to eternal life, which He gives.
options.note: Verse 27 redirects them from bread that perishes to the eternal food only He gives.

options.text: More bread to satisfy their hunger.
options.note: Jesus rebukes them precisely for coming because they ate their fill, verse 26.

options.text: A political king who would provide for their needs.
options.note: The crowd wanted to make Him king by force, but Jesus turns them from that to eternal food.

options.text: To believe in the One God has sent, the work of God.
options.note: True, and Jesus says this in verse 29, but the direct answer to what they should seek is the food that endures to eternal life.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by I am the bread of life?

options.text: He Himself is the true bread from heaven; those who come to Him and believe will never hunger and have eternal life.
options.note: Verse 35 defines the image: coming and believing satisfy forever and give life.

options.text: He provides literal bread whenever people are hungry.
options.note: The point is spiritual; He is the bread, received by faith, not a source of endless literal loaves.

options.text: He is greater than the manna Moses gave.
options.note: True, He surpasses the manna, but the fuller claim is that He Himself is the bread of life to be received by faith.

options.text: He offers wisdom and teaching that nourish the soul.
options.note: His words give life, but the claim is that He Himself, received by faith, is the life-giving bread.

quiz.q: What does eating Jesus' flesh and drinking His blood chiefly mean?

options.text: Receiving the crucified Christ by faith, so that He gives eternal life.
options.note: The discourse equates it with coming and believing, verses 35, 47, and 63.

options.text: A literal, cannibalistic consuming of His body.
options.note: Jesus says the Spirit gives life and the flesh profits nothing; the language is not literal cannibalism.

options.text: Sharing in His death and its benefits.
options.note: True and central, but it stops short of naming faith as the means by which we receive Him.

options.text: Merely admiring His sacrificial example.
options.note: It is far more than admiration; it is a living reception of Christ Himself by faith.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus say some do not come to Him even after seeing the signs?

options.text: Because no one can come unless the Father draws and grants it, and some do not believe.
options.note: Verses 44, 64, and 65 ground coming to Christ in the Father's gracious drawing.

options.text: Because the signs were not impressive enough.
options.note: The signs were clear; the issue is unbelief and the need for the Father's drawing.

options.text: Because they did not understand His teaching intellectually.
options.note: The deeper problem is unbelief and the lack of the Father's granting, not mere intellectual confusion.

options.text: Because faith is a gift that the Father must grant.
options.note: True, but it omits the paired reality that some simply did not believe, which the text also states.

quiz.q: How does Peter respond when many disciples turn back?

options.text: Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life; we believe You are the Holy One of God.
options.note: Verses 68 and 69 record Peter's confession on behalf of the Twelve.

options.text: He quietly considers leaving as well.
options.note: The opposite; Peter confesses there is nowhere else to go but to Christ.

options.text: He affirms that Jesus has the words of eternal life.
options.note: True and central, but it leaves out his fuller confession that Jesus is the Holy One of God.

options.text: He insists the teaching was easy to accept.
options.note: The teaching was hard; Peter's point is not its ease but that Christ alone has the words of life.

questions: The crowd followed Jesus for bread that perishes. What perishing things do you find yourself seeking from Jesus, rather than seeking Him as the bread of life?
Jesus said whoever comes to Me I will never drive away. How does that promise meet you if you feel unworthy or unsure of your welcome?
Feeding on Christ means receiving Him by faith day by day. What would it look like this week to feed on Jesus rather than merely admire Him?
When the teaching got hard, many turned back, but Peter said, to whom would we go? Where is Jesus asking you to keep following even when His words are difficult?
The Father promises that the Son will lose none He is given and will raise them at the last day. How does that security change the way you face doubt and death?

translationNotes.note: Three or four miles renders the Greek measure; the BSB footnote gives about twenty-five or thirty stadia, roughly 2.87 to 3.45 miles. The detail underscores that they were far from shore when Jesus came walking on the sea.
He gave them bread from heaven to eat quotes Psalm 78:24 (see also Exodus 16:4), the manna tradition that frames the whole discourse.
They will all be taught by God quotes Isaiah 54:13, which Jesus links to the Father's drawing of people to Himself.
The Holy One of God; the BSB footnote records a variant in some manuscripts, You are the Christ, the Son of the living God. Either reading is a high confession of faith.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 6:1-71. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes in verses 19 (walking on the sea) and 45 (comes to Me) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
